text.heading: Secrecy oath: Errant INEC employees to lose pay, job
text.p: The jobs of workers of the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) who refuse to swear to an oath of secrecy may be on the line, it was learnt yesterday.
INEC management, sources told The Nation in Abuja, has asked the workers to take the oath or lose their jobs for not doing so.
Besides, any errant worker may not get this month’s salary.
On June 1, INEC ordered the workers to take the oath after the lid blew open over an alleged N250million bribe during the re-run governorship election in Ekiti State.
Some INEC staff, who served as electoral officers, were said to have been given the bribe to rig the election in favour of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP).
The oath, which bars all the staff from either transmitting or disclosing any classified matter, says no staff can release any information unless he or she is permitted either by INEC or the law.
But due to ripples caused by the directive and alleged reluctance of some staff to take the oath, INEC management decided to read the Riot Act.
An INEC senior management staff, who spoke in confidence, said: "We have been told to either take the oath or lose our employment by INEC. It is now a matter of intimidation.
"It has even reached a ridiculous extent that the management said those who have refused to go to court for the administration of the oath will not be paid salaries from June ending.
"With the latest coercion, most of us have no choice than to take the oath although we are not committed to it."
The source said the staff have been submitting copies of their oath to the Personnel Department for placement in their files.
He added: "Going by the tough position of the management on the oath, I doubt if any staff will refuse to take the oath. But the truth is that most of us have resolved not to abide by it."

But INEC’s Acting Director, Public Affairs, Mr Emmanuel Umenger, said: "For those who work in sensitive places like the Presidency, civil service, security agencies and INEC, taking the oath is a normal thing.
"As far as this oath is concerned, there is no string attached and the staff are not under compulsion because it is a normal thing in the civil service.
"We have taken time to explain that the oath has no bearing with the conduct of the re-run election in Ekiti State."
There were indications last night that INEC Chairman, Prof. Maurice Iwu, is scheming for one of the three surviving National Commissioners, Mr Victor Chukwuani, to succeed him.
Besides Chukwuani, other commissioners are Phillip Umeadi (jnr.) and Deji Soyebi.
Iwu is expected to leave office next year.
A top source in INEC said: "Iwu believes that the slot is meant for the South-East and the zone should retain the seat.
"Out of the three commissioners, he is more comfortable with Chukwuani, who is also presently the most senior. We have all been seeing Chukwuani as Iwu’s anointed successor in 2010.
"In fact the INEC chairman is presently out of the country on official tour of the United States (US) and Europe. But before he left, he expressly stated that Chukwuani should be in charge."
It was however gathered that most staff appear uncomfortable with Chukwuani as the next INEC chairman.
The source added: "Left to all the staff, we want the present team of commissioners to leave us in peace so that we can reorganise this commission.

"We have never had it this bad in terms of polarisation, double speak, and flawed electoral process. The 2007 poll attracted more litigations than any other general election in the history of Nigeria".
Another principal officer of INEC said: "The choice of INEC chairman is the prerogative of the President after consultation with the National Council of State.

"Naturally, the outgoing chairman may want to recommend any reliable hand, the buck stops at the President’s table. But we are in a serious state at present because we do not have a full complement of commissioners.
"Ideally, INEC management ought to have been dissolved by the President because it is now a four-man team, instead of a 13-man structure.
"We have heard of lobbying here and there for Chukwuani but his major challenge is that he is inexperienced on electoral matters.

"Apart from being a 1989 graduate, his main pedigree is the fact that he is a former aide of the former Governor of Enugu State, Dr. Chimaroke Nnamani. As a politician, I don’t think the President will take the risk to make him the chairman of INEC.
"So, the nature of the electoral reforms of the President will determine the choice of our next chairman. The crisis in INEC now is certainly beyond ethnic coloration."
